name: Elgato 4K60 Pro MK.2 Internal Capture Card

specs.Passthrough: 4K60 HDR10 zero-lag HDMI passthrough to TV
specs.Encoding: Hardware H.264/H.265 encoding — low CPU load

whatWorks: The PCIe interface is the 4K60 Pro MK.2's fundamental advantage over external capture cards. By connecting directly to your motherboard's PCIe bus, it bypasses USB 3.0 bandwidth limitations entirely and delivers the full 4K60 HDR10 data stream without compression artifacts or frame drops. For content creators building a dedicated streaming PC, this is the technically superior approach.

Zero-lag HDMI passthrough means your gaming experience is completely unaffected by the capture process. The signal from your console or PC goes directly to your TV at full 4K60 HDR10 quality while the capture card simultaneously records or streams the game. There is no additional latency, no quality degradation — your gaming display sees exactly what it would without the capture card in the chain.

Hardware H.265 encoding offloads the compression work from your CPU to dedicated silicon on the card itself. This means encoding overhead is minimal even during 4K60 capture — your streaming PC's processor remains available for other tasks like running chat bots, overlays, or browser tabs without performance degradation on the output stream.

4K Capture Utility software is straightforward and purpose-built, but the real compatibility strength is OBS integration. The Elgato 4K60 Pro MK.2 appears as a standard capture device in OBS, XSplit, and Streamlabs without any special configuration. Content creators who already have their streaming software configured can add the card without rebuilding their workflow.
worthKnowing: This is an internal PCIe card requiring a desktop PC with an available PCIe × 4 slot. It is entirely incompatible with laptops and does not offer any external connectivity option. If your streaming setup uses a laptop, or if you need to move between rooms, the Elgato HD60 X external card is the correct product.

Windows-only — the 4K60 Pro MK.2 does not support macOS. Elgato's external USB-C cards like the HD60 X support Mac, but the PCIe internal card is a Windows ecosystem product exclusively. Mac users who want 4K capture should look at the external lineup instead.

The HDMI passthrough caps at 4K60 HDR10, which covers PS5 and Xbox Series X at their primary output modes. However, it does not support HDMI 2.1 features like 4K120 or VRR passthrough. Gamers who play at 4K120 on an HDMI 2.1 TV will see their TV quality limited to 4K60 when the capture card is in the HDMI chain.

Software capture through 4K Capture Utility supports local recording in H.264 or H.265 at up to 4K60. For streamers who also want to archive local recordings alongside their stream, a fast NVMe drive is recommended — 4K60 H.264 files can reach 50+ GB per hour depending on bitrate settings.

commonComplaints.title: No HDMI 2.1 passthrough
commonComplaints.body: The 4K60 passthrough cap means gamers who have upgraded to HDMI 2.1 TVs for 4K120 gaming cannot maintain their TV's full capability with this card in the chain. It covers the current PS5 and Xbox standard use case but will be a bottleneck as 4K120 becomes more common.

commonComplaints.title: Windows only — no Mac support
commonComplaints.body: Several content creators who run Mac-based production setups purchased this card expecting macOS compatibility and discovered it is Windows exclusive. Elgato's product page makes this clear, but the disclaimer is easy to overlook in marketplace listings.

commonComplaints.title: Requires dedicated streaming PC
commonComplaints.body: Installing a PCIe capture card requires a dedicated streaming or gaming PC with an available PCIe slot. Single-PC streamers should consider whether the motherboard has bandwidth to share between the GPU, capture card, and NVMe drive without performance impact.

commonComplaints.title: 4K Capture Utility has limited features
commonComplaints.body: The bundled 4K Capture Utility software is functional but basic compared to third-party options. Users who want advanced scene management, alerts, and overlay integration will need to pair it with OBS or Streamlabs regardless.

verdict: The Elgato 4K60 Pro MK.2 is the gold standard for internal PCIe capture cards, delivering true 4K60 HDR10 capture with zero-lag passthrough and hardware encoding that keeps CPU overhead minimal. The PCIe form factor limits it to desktop Windows PCs, and the lack of HDMI 2.1 is a meaningful future-proofing gap, but for dual-PC streaming setups targeting PS5 and Xbox Series X content at 4K60, it remains the most reliable and capable internal capture solution available.

realBuyers.quote: Installed it in my streaming PC and the quality difference versus my old HD60 S is immediately visible. 4K60 footage looks broadcast quality. My YouTube numbers improved after the upgrade.

The passthrough latency is genuinely zero. I game on my main TV with zero lag while the card captures everything on the streaming PC. It is the only capture setup I have used where gaming feels completely unaffected.
OBS integration is seamless — it appeared as a capture device immediately with no special configuration. The hardware encoding keeps my CPU at reasonable levels even during 4K60 streams.
The HDR10 passthrough is the feature I use most. My PS5 outputs Dolby Vision to my TV while the capture card records SDR for streaming. Both work simultaneously without any compromise on either side.

sources.title: Elgato 4K60 Pro MK.2 Review — Digital Trends

metaDescription: Elgato 4K60 Pro MK.2 review: The best internal PCIe capture card for 4K60 HDR10 streaming with zero-lag passthrough. $249 CAD on Amazon.ca.
introText: The Elgato 4K60 Pro MK.2 (ASIN B07WZML5GN) is available on Amazon.ca for $249 CAD and is the internal PCIe capture card of choice for dual-PC streaming setups that need true 4K60 HDR10 capture with hardware H.265 encoding and zero-lag HDMI passthrough. The card installs directly into a PCIe × 4 slot and supports OBS, XSplit, and Streamlabs out of the box.
